phrases.cultural_note: A short, punchy question perfectly suited for a loud venue. French speakers at concerts and clubs use this exact phrasing — it's natural and gets straight to the point.

responses.french: Ce soir, c'est un groupe de blues local.
responses.english: Tonight it's a local blues band.
responses.phonetic: suh SWAR, say un GROOP duh BLOO lo-KAL

responses.french: Il y a deux groupes ce soir — le premier commence à neuf heures.
responses.english: There are two bands tonight — the first starts at nine.
responses.phonetic: eel ee ah duh GROOP suh SWAR — luh pruh-MYAY koh-MONS ah nuh-VEUR

phrases.intent_en: Order a cocktail at the bar
phrases.text_local: Je voudrais un cocktail, s'il vous plaît. Qu'est-ce que vous recommandez ?
phrases.pronunciation: juh voo-DRAY un KOK-tel, seel voo PLAY. kess kuh voo ruh-koh-mon-DAY
phrases.cultural_note: Asking for a recommendation ('Qu'est-ce que vous recommandez ?') is a smart move at any bar or restaurant in France — it shows trust in the staff and often leads to discovering something excellent.

responses.french: Je vous recommande notre spécialité maison.
responses.phonetic: juh voo ruh-koh-MON no-truh spay-syah-lee-TAY may-ZON

responses.french: Le gin tonic est très populaire ce soir.
responses.english: The gin and tonic is very popular tonight.
responses.phonetic: luh JHEEN TO-neek ay tray po-poo-LAIR suh SWAR

phrases.cultural_note: 'Excellent' in French is a strong compliment — sharper and more precise than 'bien'. At a concert, you can also use 'génial' (awesome) or 'incroyable' (incredible) for even more enthusiasm.

phrases.intent_en: Ask how late the venue stays open
phrases.cultural_note: French bars and music venues often stay open later than American ones. Asking closing time ('jusqu'à quelle heure') is a practical and common question — especially if you're enjoying the night.

responses.french: Nous fermons à deux heures du matin.
responses.english: We close at two in the morning.
responses.phonetic: noo fair-MON ah duh-ZEUR doo ma-TAN

phrases.text_local: Est-ce que vous avez une carte, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: ess kuh voo ZAH-vay oon KART, seel voo PLAY
phrases.cultural_note: In France, 'la carte' is the menu (as opposed to 'le menu' which means the fixed-price meal). Asking for 'la carte' at a bar means you want to see the drinks list — very natural usage.

responses.french: Bien sûr, voilà notre carte des boissons.
responses.english: Of course, here's our drinks menu.
responses.phonetic: byan SOOR, vwah-LAH no-truh KART day bwah-SON

phrases.intent_en: Compliment the atmosphere of the venue
phrases.text_local: Quelle super ambiance ici ! J'aime beaucoup cet endroit.
phrases.pronunciation: kel SOO-pair om-BYONS ee-SEE ! jhem boh-KOO set on-DRWAH
phrases.cultural_note: 'Ambiance' is a word the English language borrowed from French — hearing it used in its original language feels authentic. 'Endroit' (place/spot) is a casual, affectionate way to refer to a venue.
